factors.label: Federal Reserve policy
factors.why: A dovish Fed normally lowers expected real yields and weakens the dollar, both supportive for gold. A hawkish Fed does the reverse.

factors.label: US real yields
factors.why: Gold pays no income, so falling inflation-adjusted bond yields reduce its opportunity cost. This is one of gold's most important macro drivers.

factors.label: US dollar (DXY)
factors.why: Gold is priced in dollars. A weaker dollar generally makes gold cheaper for non-dollar buyers and supports XAUUSD.

factors.label: Geopolitical risk
factors.why: Escalating conflict and sanctions can create safe-haven demand. The effect is strongest tactically and should not override rates indefinitely.

factors.label: Inflation trend
factors.why: Inflation affects gold through both purchasing-power demand and the policy response. The model emphasizes whether inflation changes the path of real rates.

factors.label: Growth & labour data
factors.why: Weak growth and labour data can increase easing and recession probabilities, while resilient data can keep policy restrictive.

factors.label: Central-bank demand
factors.why: Persistent official-sector accumulation creates a structural bid and reflects reserve diversification away from traditional currencies.

factors.label: 10Y Treasury yield
factors.why: Nominal yields confirm the rate environment and gold's opportunity cost, though they receive less weight than real yields to avoid double counting.

factors.label: Risk sentiment (VIX)
factors.why: Rising fear can support safe-haven demand. During acute liquidity stress, gold may initially be sold to raise cash, so this remains a secondary factor.

factors.label: ETF flows & positioning
factors.why: Investor positioning can reinforce or resist the macro regime. The live implementation uses GLD price-volume momentum as a transparent proxy, not claimed fund-flow data.
